r a CMOS-compatible direct-gap semiconductor is driving
a a surge of  interest  in 2H-Ge as a light-emitter for Si
photonics.  It is therefore critical to develop a detailed
understanding of radiative recombination in 2H-Ge, to
interpret experimental measurements and inform devel-
opment of this nascent material.
In this Article, we analyze radiative recombination in
2H-Ge via first-principles calculations. We compute the
spontaneous emission (SE) rate, which we compare to
6

plicitly compute the radiative recombination coefficient

8
transition drives significant enhancement of B. The SE
calculations corroborate that measured PL from 2H-Ge
NWs corresponds to radiative recombination across the
fundamental (direct) band gap.  However, our analysis
suggests that the initial assumption of purely radiative
recombination in 2H-Ge, which underpinned inference of
a high B coefficient, is not consistent with theory. Cal-
culated τrad values suggest that the measured carrier life-
time is dominated by as-yet unquantified recombination
mechanisms. Application of uniaxial tension along the
[0001] NW axis can enhance B by up to three orders of
magnitude, making it comparable to that in a conven-
tional (cubic) direct-gap III-V semiconductor.

B and lifetime τrad, and quantify their dependence on
carrier density n and temperature T . We also investigate
the impact of [0001] uniaxial tension on B and τrad, con-
firming that a strain-induced pseudo-direct- to direct-gap
8
transition drives significant enhancement of B. The SE
calculations corroborate that measured PL from 2H-Ge
NWs corresponds to radiative recombination across the
fundamental (direct) band gap.  However, our analysis
suggests that the initial assumption of purely radiative
recombination in 2H-Ge, which underpinned inference of
a high B coefficient, is not consistent with theory. Cal-
culated τrad values suggest that the measured carrier life-
time is dominated by as-yet unquantified recombination
mechanisms. Application of uniaxial tension along the
[0001] NW axis can enhance B by up to three orders of
magnitude, making it comparable to that in a conven-
tional (cubic) direct-gap III-V semiconductor.
Unstrained 2H-Ge
We employ the band structure and momentum ma-
trix elements calculated using density functional theory
(DFT) – via the Tran-Blaha modified Becke-Johnson
(TB-mBJ) exchange potential – to compute SE spectra,
with the B coefficient then computed via the integrated
SE rate (cf. Methods).

Vertical arrows denote the calculated band-gap energy at each
of the 2H-Ge band gap up to room temperature, we in-
clude a T -dependent band gap in our DFT calculations.
We achieve this by treating the Becke-Roussel mixing
parameter cBR in the TB-mBJ exchange potential as an
empirical parameter, which we fit to the experimental
T -dependent fundamental (indirect) L6c-Γ8v band gap of
9
conventional cubic 3C-Ge. Keeping this fit cBR(T) fixed,
we then apply it to predict the T -dependent band gap of
2H-Ge.  The results of these calculations are shown in
Fig. 1(a). Our calculated T = 0 K (cBR = 1.215) 2H-Ge
band gap of 0.360 eV is in excellent quantitative agree-
ment with experiment, exceeding the measured band gap
by only 8 meV. This simple treatment implicitly encapsu-
lates the lattice thermal expansion and electron-phonon
coupling that drive the reduction of Eg with increasing
T , accurately capturing the measured T -dependent band
gap of 2H-Ge.  Figure 1(b) shows the calculated band
structure of 2H-Ge, with cBR (= 1.185) chosen to corre-
spond to T = 300 K, where our calculated Eg = 0.281 eV
exceeds the measured value by only 2 meV. We note the
presence of a direct fundamental band gap, between the
Γ8c CB and Γ9v VB extrema, with the former originat-
ing from back-folding of the L6c CB minimum of 3C-Ge
4,5
(visible in the lowest CB along ∆ in Fig. 1(b)).
To validate our analysis, we compare calculated SE
spectra rsp(ℏω) for unstrained 2H-Ge to the PL mea-
surements of Ref. 6. The NWs of Ref. 6 have diameters
in excess of 150 nm, such that no quantum confinement
effects are expected to impact the optoelectronic prop-
erties, thereby allowing to compare our bulk DFT cal-
culations to experiment. The results of this qualitative
comparison are summarized in Fig. 1(c), for T = 4 – 300
K. Beginning at T = 4 K we adjust the carrier density
n so that the calculated SE peak energy aligns with the
17  −3
measured PL peak energy, yielding n = 3 × 10  cm  .

secant lineshape used to compute rsp(ℏω) (cf. Methods),

−2

the latter were extracted via Lasher-Stern-Wu¨rfel fits to
6
the measured PL spectra.  Similarly, the SE peak at tem-
perature T is blueshifted with respect to Eg(T) (vertical
arrows, Fig. 1(c)) due to the Burstein-Moss effect. The
calculated SE spectra track the measured PL spectra ac-
curately with increasing T , capturing the redshift of the
PL peak and the emergence of a high-energy tail driven
by the increasing carrier temperature.

cm  ).  3C-InAs is chosen as a reference material due
to its possessing a narrow, direct band gap (= 0.354 eV
11
at T = 300 K ) comparable to the pseudo-direct fun-
damental band gap of 2H-Ge.

(lines) of Fig. 2(b), we compute the radiative lifetime
−1

15  −3

5

8

As T increases, our calculated non-degenerate
τrad increases strongly, reaching 135 ms at T = 300 K.
This diverges strongly from Refs. 5 and 8, which pre-
dicted a T -independent τrad up to T ≈ 400 K. We note
that the T independence predicted in Refs. 5 and 8 is a
consequence of employing Maxwell-Boltzmann statistics,
which artificially pins the quasi-Fermi  levels by enforc-
ing unit occupancy at the band extrema – i.e. generat-
ing an explicit change of n as T is varied. The use of
Maxwell-Boltzmann statistics therefore precludes reliable
−1
T -dependent prediction of B = (τrad n)  . This contrasts
with the use of Fermi-Dirac statistics in the present anal-
ysis, which allows to independently vary n and T . At
T = 300 K we expect that τrad exceeds its T = 4 K value
+2.18
by a factor B(4 K)/B(300 K), with τrad ∝ T  at fixed
n in 2H-Ge. The strong increase of τrad with increasing
T arises in our analysis due to the decrease (increase) in
electron (hole) quasi-Fermi level, relative to the CB min-
15
imum (VB maximum), required to maintain n = 10
−3
cm  with increasing T . The calculated τrad at fixed T
decreases strongly with increasing n, reaching a value of
18  −3
17.4 µs (162.8 µs) at T = 4 (300) K for n = 10  cm  .
We note that these calculated radiative lifetimes ex-
ceed the measured 2H-Ge carrier lifetime, τ = 0.98 (0.46)
6
ns at T = 4 (300) K,  by approximately four orders
of magnitude. We recall that our calculated SE spec-.

We now turn our attention to the possibility of increas-
ing B (decreasing τrad) via strain engineering. Suckert et
8
al.  predicted theoretically that application of [0001] uni-
axial tension to 2H-Ge shifts the pseudo-direct Γ8c-Γ9v
(direct Γ7c-Γ9v) band gap upwards (downwards) in en-
ergy. This produces a pseudo-direct- to direct-gap transi-
tion for tensile c-axis strain ϵzz close to 2%, such that the
fundamental band gap becomes optically bright, leading
8
to a strong reduction of τrad. We note that, due to the
close structural similarity of [111] 3C-Ge and [0001] 2H-
Ge, this transition corresponds to the known ability to
drive an indirect- to direct-gap transition in cubic 3C-Ge
21
via uniaxial [111] tension.
Figure 3 shows the calculated band gaps of 2H-Ge
under [0001] uniaxial tension for (a) an idealized case
in which applying a c-axis strain in isolation produces
lattice parameter c = (1 + ϵzz) c0, and (b) the physi-
cally realistic case in which the uniaxial stress applied to
bring about the same extension of c is accompanied by
relaxation of the c-plane lattice parameter a and internal
parameter u.  Case (b) is equivalent to pseudomorphic
growth of compressively strained 2H-Ge on an [0001]-
oriented substrate, where lattice relaxation in response to
c-plane compression produces tension along [0001]. In the
unrelaxed case we identify a pseudo-direct- (solid blue)
to direct-gap (dashed blue) transition for ϵzz = 1.7%,
8
confirming the prediction of Suckert et al.  Comparing
Figs. 3(a) and 3(b) we observe that lattice relaxation
drives a more rapid downward shift in energy of the U5c
CB minima (dash-dotted red), leading to the emergence
of an indirect-gap regime for a narrow range of tensile
strains, ϵzz = 2.1 – 2.3%, coinciding with the pseudo-
direct Γ8c to direct Γ7c crossover at Γ, beyond which the
band gap is direct. This contrasts with Ref. 8, in which
an indirect-gap regime was not observed in TB-mBJ DFT
calculations employing self-consistent evaluation of cBR.
Our test calculations utilizing this approach confirm its
underestimation of  the experimental band gap of un-
5,8
strained 2H-Ge by ≈ 15%,  and suggests that Fig. 3(c)
in Ref. 8 may correspond to the unrelaxed case.  Our
analysis suggests that Brillouin zone- (BZ-) edge U-valley
CB states can lie close in energy to the CB minimum in
direct-gap tensile strained 2H-Ge. From the perspective
of LED/laser operation, engineering the Γ8c-U5c CB val-
ley splitting then becomes an important consideration:
the highly degenerate U valleys present a large density
of states, which can drive rapid intervalley scattering of
Γ-point electrons to the BZ edge.
The results of our strain-dependent analysis are sum-
marized in Fig. 4, where we restrict our attention to the
relaxed case of Fig. 3(b). Figure 4(a) shows the calcu-
lated B coefficient of 2H-Ge vs. ϵzz at T = 300 K in

In summary, we analyzed radiative recombination in
lonsdaleite Ge from first principles, including the B coef-
ficient and its dependence on temperature, carrier density
and strain. For unstrained 2H-Ge, calculated SE spectra
corroborate that experimentally observed PL from 2H-Ge
NWs is consistent with direct-gap optical emission. The
calculated B coefficient is approximately three orders of
magnitude lower than in 3C-InAs,  indicating a signif-
icant difference between the radiative lifetime τrad and
measured carrier lifetime τ , associated with as-yet un-
quantified recombination mechanisms. We confirmed the
presence of a pseudo-direct- to direct-gap transition un-
der [0001] unaxial tension, and elucidated the role played
by internal relaxation. We observed an indirect band gap
in a narrow strain range straddling this transition, with
a direct band gap emerging for tensile strains ≳ 2.3%.
This highlights CB valley splitting as an important con-
sideration for band-structure engineering of 2H-Ge. We
predict that tensile strain strongly enhances B, which can
approach that of 3C-InAs in the direct-gap regime. Anal-
ysis of additional recombination mechanisms is required
to inform further development of this emerging semicon-
ductor, with the aim of realizing a direct-gap emitter for
Si integrated photonics.

Density  functional  theory:  DFT calculations were
performed using the projector-augmented wave (PAW)
method, as implemented in the Vienna Ab-initio Simula-
VASP 22,23
tion Package (  ).  All calculations use a plane-
wave cut-off energy of 500 eV,  include spin-orbit cou-
pling, and employ PAW potentials in which the semi-core
10  10
(3d)  orbitals of Ge and (4d)  orbitals of In are treated
as valence states. BZ integration for the 2H and 3C crys-
tal phases was performed using Γ-centered 11 × 11 × 7
and 11 × 11 × 11 Monkhorst-Pack (MP) k-point grids,
respectively. The lattice parameters a0 = 3.961 ˚A and
c0 = 6.534 ˚A, and internal parameter u0 = 0.3744,
of 2H-Ge were computed in the LDA and are in good
5
quantitative agreement with previous calculations. Elec-
tronic structure calculations were performed in the meta-

C.G.VdW. and X.Z. All calculations were performed by
C.A.B, with technical support fromX.Z. and M.E.T., and
supervised by C.G.VdW. The writing of the manuscript
